1: hôy ʿăṭeret gēʾût šikkōrê ʾeprayim — "Woe to the proud crown of the drunkards of Ephraim!" The northern capital Samaria is described as a crown (ʿăṭeret) perched on the head of a fertile valley (gēʾ šĕmānîm). The crown is simultaneously their pride and their symbol of power. ṣîṣ nōḇēl ṣĕḇî tipʾartô — "a fading flower of his glorious beauty" — the ephemeral crown will wither like a flower. Historically: Samaria fell to Assyria in 722 BCE.
2: hinnēh ḥāzāq wĕʾammîṣ lāʾāḏōnāy — "the LORD has one who is mighty and strong" — Assyria is YHWH's instrument. The imagery: a driving storm of hail (zerem bārāḏ saʿar qāṭeb) and flooding waters (zerem mayim kabbîrîm šōṭĕpîm). The natural forces are weaponized as divine judgment. The proud crown of Ephraim (Samaria) will be overwhelmed by the very forces that seemed to surround it safely.
3: bĕreglāyim tĕrûmmanâ ʿăṭeret gēʾût šikkōrê ʾeprayim — "the proud crown of the drunkards of Ephraim will be trampled underfoot" — the literal trampling fulfills the metaphor of Isaiah 28:1-2. The crown of pride (ʿăṭeret gēʾût) becomes refuse underfoot — the reversal from head-covering to foot-contact is total humiliation.
4: wĕhāyĕtâ ṣîṣat nōḇēl ṣĕḇî tipʾartô — "the fading flower of his glorious beauty" repeats Isaiah 28:1's image but adds the resolution: it will be like a first-ripe fig (bikkûrâ) before the summer, which the one who sees it immediately swallows (biltĕʿennāh). The first fig of the season is devoured on sight — the eagerness of the destroyer matches the sudden vulnerability of the city.
5: bayyôm hahûʾ yihyeh Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt lĕʿăṭeret ṣĕbî — "on that day YHWH of hosts will be a crown of beauty" (ʿăṭeret ṣĕbî) — the divine substitute for the human proud crown of Ephraim. YHWH himself becomes the crown, the glory, and the diadem (ṣĕpîrat tipʾārâ) for the remnant of his people (lišĕʾār ʿammô). The same imagery used to describe Samaria's pride is redirected to describe YHWH's presence with the faithful remnant.
6: rûaḥ mišpāṭ lĕyôšēḇ hammišpāṭ — "a spirit of justice to the one who sits in judgment" — the judicial function rightly discharged when YHWH is the crown; ûgĕḇûrâ lĕmĕšîḇê milḥāmâ šāʿrâ — "strength to those who turn back the battle at the gate." The gate was both the court and the last line of defense — YHWH's presence enables both true justice and effective defense.
7: Now Isaiah turns to Judah and Jerusalem: wĕgam-ʾēlleh bayyāyin šāgû — "but even these stagger with wine" (šāgâ, H7686, to go astray, err, stagger). The same drunkard imagery applied to Ephraim now indicts Jerusalem. The priest (kōhēn) and the prophet (nāḇîʾ) specifically are drunk — those responsible for justice and revelation have compromised their office through intoxication and thus error in vision (bāreʾâ pāqû) and judgment (kāšĕlû bammišpāṭ).
8: kî kol-šulḥānôt māleʾû qîʾ ṣōʾâ bĕlî māqôm — "for all tables are full of vomit and filth" — the graphic image of a banquet hall defiled by the drunkenness of those who ate there. The prophetic indictment uses the physical reality of the consequence of drunkenness to symbolize the moral-spiritual state of the leadership: what should be the table of holy counsel and sacred meal has become a site of degradation.
9: The mocking response of the drunken leaders to Isaiah: ʾet-mî yôreh ḏēʿâ — "whom will he teach knowledge?" and ʾet-mî yāḇîn šĕmûʿâ — "to whom will he explain the message?" — the sarcastic questions imply: does he think we are children, just weaned from milk (gĕmûlê mēḥālāḇ ʿattîqê miššādāyim)? The leaders reject the simplicity of YHWH's instruction as beneath their sophistication.
10: kî ṣaw lāṣaw ṣaw lāṣaw qaw lāqaw qaw lāqaw — the debated phrase: either (a) the leadership mocks Isaiah's teaching as baby-talk (ṣaw and qaw as meaningless syllables), or (b) Isaiah describes YHWH's patient repetitive instruction (line upon line, precept upon precept — zĕʿēr šām zĕʿēr šām). The ambiguity may be intentional: what the leaders mock as childish repetition is actually YHWH's gracious teaching method.
11: kî bĕlaʿăgê śāpâ ûblĕšôn ʾaḥeret yĕdabbēr ʾel hāʿam hazzeh — "for by people of strange lips and a foreign tongue he will speak to this people" — YHWH will use the incomprehensible language of the Assyrian army as his word to the people who would not hear clear Hebrew instruction. Paul quotes this verse in 1 Corinthians 14:21 as a text about tongues (glossolalia) — the sign of tongues addressed to those who do not believe.
12: ʾăšer ʾāmar ʾălêhem zōʾt hammĕnûḥâ hānîḥû lĕʿāyēp — "who said to them, 'This is the resting place; let the weary rest'" — YHWH offered rest (mĕnûḥâ) through his instruction, but they would not hear. wĕlōʾ ʾāḇû šĕmôaʿ — "and they were not willing to listen." The refusal of offered rest is a persistent Isaianic motif (cf. Isaiah 30:15: "in returning and rest you shall be saved") that Hebrews 3-4 develops as the warning against refusing the Sabbath-rest in Christ.
13: The consequence: the word of YHWH (dĕbar Yhwh) that they refused now comes to them as ṣaw lāṣaw ṣaw lāṣaw — the very thing they mocked becomes the instrument of their stumbling (kāšĕlû wĕnāplû), being broken (wĕnišbārû), trapped (wĕnôqĕšû), and taken (wĕnillākĕdû). The four verbs of destruction progressively increase in finality.
14: lākēn šimʿû dĕbar Yhwh ʾanšê lāṣôn — "therefore hear the word of YHWH, you scoffers (lāṣôn, H3944, those who mock/deride)" — the address to the leaders of Jerusalem who have struck a deal with Egypt against Assyria. The term ʾanšê lāṣôn (men of scoffing/mockery) identifies the ruling class's defining posture toward prophetic warning.
15: kāratnû bĕrît ʾet-māwet wĕʿim-šĕʾôl ʿāśînû ḥōzeh — "we have made a covenant with death, and with Sheol we have an agreement (ḥōzeh)." The Jerusalem leaders' claim — that they have a deal that will protect them from the coming "overflowing scourge" (šôṭ šôṭēp) by hiding in lies (šeqer maḥsēnû) and falsehood (bāšāqer nistaranû). The political alliance with Egypt is metaphorized as a covenant with death itself.
16: hinnēh yissaḏ bĕṣiyyôn ʾāben ʾeben bōḥan pinnāt yiqrat mûssāḏ mûssāḏ — "Behold, I am laying in Zion a stone, a tested/proven stone, a precious cornerstone as a sure foundation." The cornerstone that YHWH lays contrasts with the false foundation the leaders have built on lies. hammaʾămîn lōʾ yāḥîš — "the one who believes/trusts will not flee/be in haste." This verse is cited in Romans 9:33; 1 Peter 2:6-8; Matthew 21:42 — the NT identifies Christ as this tested cornerstone.
17: wĕśamtî mišpāṭ lĕqāw ûṣĕdāqâ lĕmišqālet — "I will make justice the measuring line (qāw) and righteousness the plumb line (mišqālet)" — the precise measurement of mišpāṭ and ṣĕdāqâ will expose the false foundation. The same qāw word used mockingly in Isaiah 28:10,13 now becomes the divine measuring instrument. yissaḥ bārāḏ māḥăseh kāzāb — "hail will sweep away the refuge of lies" — the covenant with death is undone.
18: wĕkuppar bĕrîtĕkem ʾet-māwet — "your covenant with death will be annulled/covered" (kāpar, the atonement/covering verb used here for cancellation). The overflowing scourge (šôṭ šôṭēp) will come and they will be trampled by it (wahyîtem lô lĕmirmās). The false security is stripped away: every time the scourge passes it will take them (yiqqaḥ ʾetkem).
19: miggap ʿôbĕrô yiqqaḥ ʾetkem — "each time it passes, it will take you" — the repeated assault. kî-baqqer baqqer yaʿăbōr bayyôm wāballaylâ — "morning by morning it will pass, by day and by night." The relentlessness of the judgment is its defining quality. raq šĕmûʿâ hăḇîn ʾet-haššĕmûʿâ — "it will be nothing but terror to understand the message" — the very understanding of what YHWH has said will itself be terrifying.
20: kî qāṣar hammittâ mihistārēaʿ — "for the bed is too short to stretch out on" — the proverbial saying (too short to lie in, too narrow to wrap in) describes the inadequacy of the false security. The Egyptian alliance is like a bed that is too small and a blanket that is too narrow — it cannot actually cover the danger or provide the promised rest.
21: kî kĕhar pĕrāṣîm yāqûm Yhwh — "for YHWH will rise up as at Mount Perazim" (2 Samuel 5:20, where YHWH broke out against the Philistines for David); kĕʿēmeq bĕgiḇʿôn yirgeʿ — "as in the valley of Gibeon he will storm" (probably Joshua 10:10-11). maʿăśēhû zār maʿăśēhû — "his work is strange/alien, his deed is foreign" — judgment against his own people Israel/Judah is unnatural to YHWH; mercy and restoration are his characteristic work. Discipline is the exception, not the norm.
22: wĕʿattâ ʾal-titṣaḥăqû — "therefore do not scoff" — the direct warning to the scoffers addressed in Isaiah 28:14. The restraint from scoffing is grounded in the fact that YHWH of hosts has told him (kī kālâ wĕneḥĕrāṣâ šāmaʿtî mēʾēt ʾăḏōnāy Yhwh ṣĕḇāʾôt ʿal-kol-hāʾāreṣ) "a decreed end/destruction upon the whole earth." The cosmic scope of what has been determined makes mockery both foolish and dangerous.
23: haʾăzînû ûšimʿû qôlî haqšîḇû ûšimʿû ʾimrātî — "give ear and hear my voice; be attentive and hear what I say" — the introduction to the agricultural parable (Isaiah 28:24-29). The call to attentive listening (haʾăzîn, šāmaʿ, hiqšîḇ — three hearing verbs) signals that what follows requires careful thought, not surface reading.
24: The farmer does not plow all day to prepare the ground — he plows in order to plant (yiphattaḥ wîśadded ʾaḏmātô, he opens and breaks up his ground). hal kol-hayyôm yaḥărōš haḥōrēš — "does the plowman plow all day?" The rhetorical question expects "no" — preparation serves a purpose beyond itself. The parable will draw an analogy to divine action: YHWH's destructive work (plowing) serves a constructive purpose (harvest).
25: After preparing the ground, the farmer sows in order: qeṣaḥ (dill/black cumin) and kammōn (cumin) are scattered; ḥiṭṭâ (wheat) and śĕʿōrâ (barley) are planted in rows; kussemeth (spelt/emmer) at the border. Each seed has its own proper place (nismān śĕʿōrâ ûkussemeth gebûlātô) — the farmer arranges according to the nature of each crop. The specificity of agricultural knowledge here reflects real Palestinian farming practice.
26: wĕyissĕrô lammišpāṭ — "and he instructs him for the right order/justice" — YHWH is the source of the farmer's knowledge. ʾĕlōhāyw yôrennû — "his God teaches him." The agricultural wisdom of the skilled farmer is itself a form of divine instruction — wisdom about the natural world and wisdom about YHWH's governance share the same source.
27: Different crops require different threshing methods: qeṣaḥ is not threshed with a threshing sledge (bĕḥārûṣ) — it is beaten out with a stick (bĕmaṭṭeh); kammōn not with a wagon wheel (ʾôpan ʿăgālâ) — with a rod (bĕšāḇeṭ). The delicate seeds require delicate methods. The implication for divine action: YHWH applies graduated, appropriate judgment, not indiscriminate force.
28: Grain is crushed but not infinitely — the thresher does not thresh it forever (lōʾ lāneṣaḥ yādûš); when he drives the wagon wheel and horses over it, he does not grind it (lōʾ yĕšōḥeqennû). The grain is threshed to free the kernel, not to destroy it. galgal ʿeglātô ûpārāšāyw lōʾ yĕdaqqennû — "he does not powder it with his wagon wheel and horses." The restraint of the divine thresher mirrors the farmer's wisdom.
29: gam-zōʾt mēʾim Yhwh ṣĕbāʾôt yāṣāʾâ — "this also comes from YHWH of hosts" — the agricultural wisdom of the parable is explicitly claimed as coming from YHWH. hipliʾ ʿēṣâ hiḡdîl tûšiyyâ — "he is wonderful in counsel (pālāʾ, the same root as in Isaiah 9:6's pele-yôʿēṣ, Wonderful Counselor), and excellent in wisdom (hiḡdîl tûšiyyâ, made great the sound wisdom)." The agricultural parable thus closes with a title that ties Isaiah 28 to Isaiah 9 — YHWH's wisdom in governing history is as careful and purposeful as a skilled farmer's wisdom in managing his harvest.
